questions.question: What is the chromatic number of an n vertex simple connected graph which does not contain any odd length cycle? Assume n >= 2. A. 2  B. 3  C. n-1  D. n
questions.to_find: Chromatic number of a connected graph with no odd cycle (n >= 2).

understand.plain: A graph with no odd-length cycle is exactly a bipartite graph - you can split its vertices into two groups with all edges going between the groups. Two groups means two colours are enough, and since it has at least one edge it needs more than one, so the chromatic number is exactly 2.

keywords.explain: the fewest colours needed so that no two adjacent vertices share a colour
keywords.example: a single edge needs 2 colours -> chi = 2

keywords.explain: a cycle with an odd number of edges, like a triangle (length 3) or a pentagon (length 5)
keywords.example: C_3 is an odd cycle and forces a 3rd colour

keywords.explain: vertices split into two sides with every edge crossing between sides; equivalent to 'has no odd cycle'
keywords.example: a path or an even cycle is bipartite

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Bipartite 2-colouring'><title>No odd cycle means bipartite means 2 colours</title><desc>A graph with no odd cycle splits into two sides; colour one side red and the other green, giving chromatic number 2.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='130' y1='60' x2='430' y2='60' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='130' y1='60' x2='430' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='130' y1='150' x2='430' y2='60' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='130' y1='150' x2='430' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='130' cy='60' r='18' class='no'/><circle cx='130' cy='150' r='18' class='no'/><circle cx='430' cy='60' r='18' class='ok'/><circle cx='430' cy='150' r='18' class='ok'/><text x='130' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Side 1 (colour 1)</text><text x='430' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Side 2 (colour 2)</text><text x='280' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>no odd cycle -> bipartite -> chi = 2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two columns of vertices with all edges crossing between them: one side coloured 1, the other coloured 2, so only 2 colours are used.

given.aim: Find the chromatic number of any connected n-vertex simple graph (n >= 2) that has no odd-length cycle.

terms.example: n >= 2 so at least one edge exists
terms.connects: rules out the trivial single-vertex (1-colour) case

terms.meaning: the graph contains no cycle of odd length
terms.connects: the defining property of bipartite graphs

terms.meaning: every vertex is reachable from every other
terms.example: guarantees at least one edge for n >= 2

given.plan: Recognise 'no odd cycle' as the characterisation of a bipartite graph, then use that a bipartite graph with at least one edge has chromatic number exactly 2.

steps.title: No odd cycle means bipartite
steps.formula_raw: G has no odd cycle  <=>  G is bipartite
steps.apply: The graph splits into two parts with all edges between them.
steps.note: This is Konig's characterisation of bipartite graphs.

steps.title: Bipartite graph with an edge needs 2 colours
steps.formula_raw: chi(G) = 2  for a bipartite graph with at least one edge
steps.apply: Colour one side with colour 1 and the other side with colour 2; since n >= 2 and G is connected there is an edge, so chi != 1.
steps.note: Lower bound 2 from the edge, upper bound 2 from the bipartition -> exactly 2.

questions.formula_note: Key idea: 'no odd cycle' is the exact definition of bipartite, and a bipartite graph with an edge is always 2-chromatic - independent of n.

questions.question: The minimum number of colours that is sufficient to vertex-colour any planar graph is ________.
questions.to_find: Colours that always suffice to properly vertex-colour every planar graph.

understand.plain: This is the famous Four Colour Theorem: any graph that can be drawn on a plane without edges crossing can have its vertices coloured with just 4 colours so that no two neighbours match. Four colours always suffice, and some planar graphs (like K_4) genuinely need all four.

keywords.explain: a graph that can be drawn in the plane with no two edges crossing
keywords.example: a map of countries as regions is planar

keywords.explain: every planar graph is 4-colourable (proved by Appel and Haken, 1976)
keywords.example: any political map needs at most 4 colours

keywords.explain: the count that works for the worst-case planar graph, not a particular one
keywords.example: K_4 is planar and needs all 4 colours

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Four colour theorem on K4'><title>Planar K4 needs 4 colours</title><desc>The planar graph K4 has every pair of its 4 vertices adjacent, so all four must get different colours, illustrating that 4 colours are both necessary and sufficient for planar graphs.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='280' y1='40' x2='150' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='280' y1='40' x2='410' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='280' y1='40' x2='280' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='150' y1='150' x2='410' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='150' y1='150' x2='280' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='410' y1='150' x2='280' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='280' cy='40' r='18' style='fill:#D85A30'/><circle cx='150' cy='150' r='18' style='fill:#1D9E75'/><circle cx='410' cy='150' r='18' style='fill:#7F77DD'/><circle cx='280' cy='130' r='18' style='fill:#E0B500'/><text x='280' y='225' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>planar K_4: all 4 mutually adjacent -> 4 colours needed</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The planar graph K4 drawn with one vertex inside the triangle of the other three; all four vertices are pairwise adjacent so each needs a distinct colour.

given.aim: State the smallest number of colours guaranteed to properly colour the vertices of every planar graph.

terms.meaning: drawable in the plane with no edge crossings
terms.connects: the class of graphs the bound must cover

terms.meaning: the hardest planar graph to colour
terms.connects: why the answer is 4, not fewer

given.plan: Recall the Four Colour Theorem - 4 colours suffice for every planar graph - and note K_4 shows 4 can be required, fixing the answer at 4.

steps.title: Apply the Four Colour Theorem
steps.apply: Every planar graph is 4-colourable, so 4 colours are always sufficient.

steps.title: Show 4 can be necessary
steps.apply: K_4 is planar and needs 4 colours, so no smaller number is sufficient for all planar graphs.
steps.note: Hence the minimum sufficient count is exactly 4.

questions.formula_note: Key fact: the Four Colour Theorem caps every planar graph at chi <= 4, and planar K_4 shows the bound is tight.

questions.question: The chromatic number of the following graph is _____. The graph has 6 vertices a, b, c, d, e, f with edges: a-e, a-b, a-c, c-e, c-d, c-b, b-d, b-f, d-f, e-f.
questions.to_find: Chromatic number of the given 6-vertex graph.

understand.plain: Look for the largest clique (group of mutually adjacent vertices) to get a lower bound, then try to actually colour the graph with that many colours. The triangle a-b-c forces at least 3 colours, and a valid 3-colouring of the whole graph exists, so the chromatic number is exactly 3.

keywords.explain: a set of vertices all pairwise adjacent; a clique of size k needs k colours
assign colours so no edge joins two same-coloured vertices
keywords.example: a=1, b=2, c=3 for the triangle

keywords.explain: the smallest number of colours in any proper colouring

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='3-colouring of the graph'><title>A valid 3-colouring</title><desc>The graph on a,b,c,d,e,f contains the triangle a-b-c forcing 3 colours; a full 3-colouring assigns a=1, b=2, c=3, d=1, e=2, f=3.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe understand.visual_alt: The 6-vertex graph coloured with 3 colours: a=1, e=2, c=3, d=1, b=2, f=3, with the triangle a-b-c using all three colours.

given.aim: Determine the smallest number of colours to properly colour the shown graph on a, b, c, d, e, f.

terms.meaning: the 6 nodes a, b, c, d, e, f
terms.example: a top-left, e top-right, b bottom-left, f bottom-right

terms.meaning: a-e, a-b, a-c, c-e, c-d, c-b, b-d, b-f, d-f, e-f

terms.example: edges a-b, b-c, a-c all present

given.plan: Find a triangle for a lower bound (chi >= 3), then exhibit an explicit 3-colouring of all six vertices to confirm chi = 3.

steps.title: Lower bound from a clique
steps.apply: Vertices a, b, c are pairwise adjacent (a-b, b-c, a-c), so they form K_3 and need 3 distinct colours: chi >= 3.
steps.note: omega(G) is the size of the largest clique.

steps.title: Exhibit a 3-colouring
steps.apply: Check each edge: a-e (1,2), a-b (1,2), a-c (1,3), c-e (3,2), c-d (3,1), c-b (3,2), b-d (2,1), b-f (2,3), d-f (1,3), e-f (2,3) - all endpoints differ.
steps.note: A valid 3-colouring shows 3 colours suffice, so chi = 3.

questions.formula_note: Key idea: sandwich chi between the clique lower bound (triangle a-b-c gives 3) and an explicit 3-colouring (gives at most 3).

questions.question: Graph G is obtained by adding vertex s to K_{3,4} and making s adjacent to every vertex of K_{3,4}. The minimum number of colours required to edge-colour G is _______.
questions.to_find: Edge chromatic number (chromatic index) of G = K_{3,4} plus a universal vertex s.

understand.plain: Edge-colouring means colouring edges so that edges meeting at a common vertex get different colours - so you need at least as many colours as the highest vertex degree. Adding s joined to all 7 vertices of K_{3,4} makes s have degree 7, the largest in the graph. By Vizing's theorem the chromatic index is the max degree or one more; here it turns out to be exactly the max degree, 7.

keywords.explain: assign colours to edges so no two edges sharing a vertex have the same colour
keywords.example: the edges at one vertex are all different colours

keywords.explain: complete bipartite graph: 3 vertices on one side joined to all 4 on the other
the fewest colours for a proper edge colouring; always Delta or Delta+1 (Vizing)

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Universal vertex s over K34'><title>Adding s adjacent to all of K_{3,4}</title><desc>K_{3,4} has parts of size 3 and 4; vertex s is joined to all 7 vertices, giving s degree 7, the maximum degree, so the edge chromatic number is 7.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe stroke-width='1.5'/><line class='edge' x1='120' y1='150' x2='480' y2='280' stroke='#D8D6CF' stroke-width='1.5'/><circle cx='300' cy='40' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='300' y='46' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>s</text><circle cx='120' cy='150' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='120' cy='200' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='120' cy='250' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='480' cy='130' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='480' cy='180' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='480' cy='230' r='14' class='bg'/><circle cx='480' cy='280' r='14' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>X (3)</text><text x='480' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Y (4)</text><text x='300' y='298' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>deg(s) = 7 = Delta(G) -> chi'(G) = 7</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Vertex s at the top joined to all 3 vertices of side X and all 4 of side Y of K_{3,4}, giving s degree 7, the maximum degree of the graph.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of colours to properly edge-colour G = K_{3,4} with an added universal vertex s.

terms.meaning: complete bipartite base graph, parts X (size 3) and Y (size 4)
terms.example: 12 edges; degrees 4 in X, 3 in Y
terms.connects: the graph s is attached to

terms.meaning: new vertex adjacent to all 7 vertices of K_{3,4}

terms.example: X-vertices reach degree 5, Y-vertices 4, s reaches 7 -> Delta = 7
terms.connects: lower bound for the chromatic index

given.plan: Compute the maximum degree of G (it is deg(s) = 7), use chi'(G) >= Delta(G), and apply Vizing's theorem; here a 7-colouring exists so chi'(G) = 7.

steps.title: Find the maximum degree
steps.apply: After adding s: X-vertices have degree 4+1=5, Y-vertices have degree 3+1=4, and s has degree 7. So Delta(G) = 7.
steps.note: s is adjacent to all 7 original vertices, giving the largest degree.

steps.title: Bound the chromatic index
steps.apply: chi'(G) >= Delta(G) = 7, and by Vizing chi'(G) is 7 or 8.
steps.note: Edges meeting at s alone force at least 7 colours.

steps.title: A 7-colouring exists
steps.apply: G has only 8 vertices and 19 edges, and one can schedule the edges into 7 matchings; G is Class 1, so chi'(G) = 7.
steps.note: Hence the minimum number of edge colours is 7.

questions.formula_note: Key idea: the added universal vertex s makes Delta = 7, and Vizing pins chi' to 7 or 8; the graph is Class 1, so the answer is exactly 7.

questions.question: Let G be a simple, finite, undirected graph with vertex set {v_1, ..., v_n}. Let Delta(G) denote the maximum degree of G and let N = {1, 2, ...} denote the set of all possible colors. Color the vertices of G using the following greedy strategy: for i = 1, ..., n: color(v_i) <- min{j in N : no neighbour of v_i is colored j}. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE? A. This procedure results in a proper vertex coloring of G.  B. The number of colors used is at most Delta(G)+1.  C. The number of colors used is at most Delta(G).  D. The number of colors used is equal to the chromatic number of G.
questions.to_find: Which statements about the greedy vertex-colouring procedure are always true.

understand.plain: The greedy procedure gives each vertex the smallest colour not already used by its earlier-coloured neighbours. By construction no neighbour can share its colour, so the result is always a proper colouring. Each vertex has at most Delta neighbours, so among colours 1..Delta+1 at least one is free - the procedure never needs more than Delta+1 colours. It can use fewer than Delta+1 but is not guaranteed to hit the true chromatic number.

keywords.explain: colour vertices one by one, each time picking the smallest colour not used by already-coloured neighbours
keywords.example: v_1 gets colour 1, then each later vertex avoids its done neighbours

keywords.explain: the maximum degree - the most neighbours any single vertex has
keywords.example: if every vertex has <= 3 neighbours, Delta = 3

keywords.explain: no edge connects two vertices of the same colour
keywords.example: always achieved here because a vertex avoids its neighbours' colours

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 560 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Greedy uses at most Delta+1 colours'><title>Greedy bound Delta+1</title><desc>When colouring a vertex with at most Delta neighbours, at most Delta colours are blocked, so one of the colours 1..Delta+1 is always free, bounding the greedy colouring by Delta+1.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='60' width='70' height='60' rx='10' class='no'/><rect x='120' y='60' width='70' height='60' rx='10' class='no'/><rect x='200' y='60' width='70' height='60' rx='10' class='no'/><rect x='300' y='60' width='80' height='60' rx='10' class='ok'/><text x='75' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><text x='155' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2</text><text x='235' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>...</text><text x='340' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>D+1</text><text x='155' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>at most Delta blocked</text><text x='340' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>free</text><text x='280' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'><= Delta neighbours -> some colour in 1..Delta+1 is free</text><text x='280' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>so greedy uses at most Delta(G)+1 colours</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A row of colours 1..Delta+1 where at most Delta are blocked by neighbours, leaving at least one free, so the greedy colouring never exceeds Delta+1 colours.

given.aim: Decide which claims about the greedy first-fit vertex colouring always hold.

terms.meaning: smallest colour not used by already-coloured neighbours of v_i
terms.example: if neighbours use 1 and 2, then color = 3
terms.connects: the rule that defines the procedure

terms.example: the most neighbours any vertex has
terms.connects: caps how many colours can be blocked at any step

terms.example: the optimum any colouring could reach
terms.connects: greedy may exceed it, so statement D fails

given.plan: Check each statement: properness is built into the rule (A true); the Delta+1 bound follows since at most Delta colours are blocked (B true); C and D fail on examples like K_n and adversarial orderings.

steps.title: A - it is always proper
steps.formula_raw: color(v_i) != color(u) for every coloured neighbour u
steps.apply: By definition v_i takes a colour no already-coloured neighbour has; later neighbours likewise avoid v_i. So no edge is monochromatic - proper. TRUE.
steps.note: Properness is guaranteed by the selection rule itself.

steps.title: B - at most Delta+1 colours
steps.apply: Each v_i has at most Delta neighbours, so at most Delta colours are blocked; among {1, ..., Delta+1} one is always free. TRUE.
steps.note: This is the standard greedy / first-fit bound.

steps.title: C and D - not always true
steps.formula_raw: C: <= Delta  fails;  D: = chi(G)  fails
steps.apply: For K_n, Delta = n-1 but n colours are needed, so C (<= Delta) is false. A bad ordering can make greedy use more than chi(G) (e.g. a crown graph), so D is false.
steps.note: Greedy is optimal only with a good ordering; in general it may overshoot chi.

solution.result: Statements A and B are TRUE (answer: A, B).

questions.formula_note: Key idea: greedy first-fit is always proper and uses at most Delta+1 colours, but it neither beats Delta nor necessarily reaches chi(G).

questions.question: The chromatic number of a graph is the minimum number of colours used in a proper colouring of the graph. Let G be any graph with n vertices and chromatic number k. Which of the following statements is/are always TRUE? A. G contains a complete subgraph with k vertices.  B. G contains an independent set of size at least n/k.  C. G contains at least k(k-1)/2 edges.  D. G contains a vertex of degree at least k.
questions.to_find: Which statements about a graph with n vertices and chromatic number k are always true.

understand.plain: Colour the graph optimally with k colours. The k colour classes partition the n vertices, so by pigeonhole the biggest class has at least n/k vertices and is independent (B). Also, between every pair of colour classes there must be at least one edge - otherwise the two classes could merge into one colour - giving at least C(k,2) = k(k-1)/2 edges (C). Statements A and D fail because high chromatic number does not force a big clique or a high-degree vertex (odd cycles are the classic counterexamples).

keywords.explain: a set of vertices with no edges among them - exactly one colour class
keywords.example: all vertices coloured 'red' form an independent set

keywords.explain: all vertices sharing one colour in a proper colouring
keywords.example: k colours partition the vertices into k classes

keywords.explain: chi >= omega always, but chi can far exceed the largest clique
keywords.example: C_5 has chi = 3 but largest clique only 2

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='k colour classes need an edge between each pair'><title>Why C(k,2) edges are forced</title><desc>The k colour classes partition n vertices; the largest class has at least n/k vertices (B), and every pair of classes needs at least one edge or they would merge, forcing at least k(k-1)/2 edges (C).</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='150' y1='90' x2='450' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='150' y1='90' x2='300' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='450' y1='90' x2='300' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='150' cy='90' r='30' class='no'/><circle cx='450' cy='90' r='30' class='ok'/><circle cx='300' cy='200' r='30' class='hi'/><text x='150' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C1</text><text x='450' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C2</text><text x='300' y='206' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C3</text><text x='300' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>>= 1 edge per pair</text><text x='300' y='255' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>k classes, each pair joined -> >= C(k,2) = k(k-1)/2 edges</text><text x='300' y='278' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>largest class >= n/k vertices (independent)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three colour classes drawn as big circles with at least one edge between every pair, illustrating the k(k-1)/2 edge bound and the n/k independent set.

given.aim: Determine which claims hold for every graph G with n vertices and chromatic number k.

terms.example: the total being partitioned by colours

terms.meaning: chromatic number = number of colour classes in an optimal colouring
terms.connects: each class is independent; pairs of classes are joined

terms.meaning: the k groups of equally-coloured vertices
terms.connects: B comes from the biggest class, C from edges between classes

given.plan: Take an optimal k-colouring: the largest class gives an independent set of size >= n/k (B); each pair of classes must share an edge or merge, giving >= C(k,2) edges (C). Disprove A and D with the odd-cycle counterexample.

steps.title: B - large independent set
steps.apply: The k colour classes partition n vertices, so by pigeonhole the largest class has >= n/k vertices, and a colour class is independent. TRUE.
steps.note: alpha(G) is the size of the largest independent set.

steps.title: C - edge lower bound
steps.apply: Between any two colour classes there must be at least one edge (else they could share a colour, contradicting chi = k). There are C(k,2) class pairs, so |E| >= k(k-1)/2. TRUE.
steps.note: This is why a graph with chi = k has at least as many edges as K_k.

steps.title: A and D - false in general
steps.apply: C_5 has chi = 3 (k=3) but no triangle, so no K_3 subgraph - A fails. Its max degree is 2 < 3 = k, so D fails too.
steps.note: Chromatic number can exceed both the clique number and the max degree.

solution.result: Statements B and C are always TRUE (answer: B, C).

questions.formula_note: Key idea: an optimal k-colouring forces a big independent set (>= n/k) and at least C(k,2) edges, but it does NOT force a K_k clique or a degree-k vertex (C_5 is the counterexample).

formulas.name: Bipartite Characterisation (no odd cycle)
formulas.latex: G \text{ is bipartite} \iff G \text{ has no odd cycle}
formulas.plain: A graph is bipartite (vertices split into two sides with all edges crossing) exactly when it contains no cycle of odd length.
formulas.whenToUse: When a problem says 'no odd cycle' or asks whether a graph is 2-colourable / bipartite.

terms.means: a closed walk using an odd number of distinct edges, e.g. C_3, C_5

formulas.trap: A single even cycle being present does not make a graph bipartite; ALL cycles must be even (equivalently, no odd cycle at all).
formulas.reference: Konig's theorem / standard graph theory characterisation of bipartite graphs.

formulas.name: Chromatic Number of a Bipartite Graph
formulas.latex: \chi(G) = 2 \text{ for a bipartite } G \text{ with at least one edge}
formulas.plain: Any bipartite graph that has at least one edge can be properly coloured with exactly 2 colours.
formulas.whenToUse: When the graph is bipartite (or equivalently has no odd cycle) and has at least one edge.

terms.means: chromatic number, the minimum number of colours
a bipartite graph with parts X and Y

formulas.trap: An edgeless graph (or a single vertex) has chi = 1, not 2; the '2' needs at least one edge.
formulas.reference: Direct consequence of the two-sided partition of a bipartite graph.

formulas.name: Four Colour Theorem
formulas.plain: Every planar graph can be properly vertex-coloured using at most 4 colours.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked for the colours that always suffice to vertex-colour any planar graph or map.

terms.means: a planar graph (drawable with no edge crossings)

formulas.trap: 4 is the sufficient count for the WORST planar graph; many planar graphs need fewer, but K_4 shows 4 can be required so 3 is not enough in general.
formulas.reference: Appel and Haken (1976), Four Colour Theorem.

formulas.name: Clique Lower Bound for Chromatic Number
formulas.plain: The chromatic number is at least the size of the largest clique, since every vertex of a clique must get a different colour.
formulas.whenToUse: To get a quick lower bound on chi by spotting a triangle or larger complete subgraph.

terms.means: clique number, size of the largest set of pairwise adjacent vertices

formulas.trap: The inequality can be strict: chi may exceed omega (e.g. C_5 has omega = 2 but chi = 3), so a clique gives only a lower bound.

formulas.name: Vizing's Theorem (Edge Chromatic Number)
formulas.plain: For any simple graph, the minimum number of colours to edge-colour it (the chromatic index) is either the maximum degree or one more than it.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked for the minimum number of colours to edge-colour a simple graph.

terms.means: chromatic index, minimum colours for a proper edge colouring

formulas.trap: It does not say which of Delta or Delta+1; you must check the structure. Bipartite graphs are always Class 1 (chi' = Delta), but general graphs may be Class 2.

formulas.name: Greedy (First-Fit) Colouring Bound
formulas.plain: Colouring vertices one by one and always choosing the smallest colour not used by already-coloured neighbours produces a proper colouring using at most Delta+1 colours.
formulas.whenToUse: When analysing the greedy / first-fit vertex colouring procedure or proving the chi <= Delta+1 bound.

terms.means: number of colours the first-fit procedure actually uses

formulas.trap: Greedy is always proper and never exceeds Delta+1, but it is NOT guaranteed to reach the true chromatic number and need not stay within Delta (K_n forces Delta+1).
formulas.reference: Standard greedy colouring argument (proof of chi <= Delta+1).

formulas.name: Independent Set Lower Bound from Chromatic Number
formulas.plain: In any proper colouring with chi colours the n vertices split into chi colour classes (each independent), so the largest one has at least n/chi vertices.
formulas.whenToUse: To bound the size of the largest independent set using the chromatic number and vertex count.

terms.means: independence number, size of the largest independent set

formulas.trap: The bound is a floor of a pigeonhole argument: alpha >= ceil(n/chi); writing it as exactly n/k can mislead when n/k is not an integer.
formulas.reference: Pigeonhole on the colour classes of an optimal colouring.

formulas.name: Edge Lower Bound from Chromatic Number
formulas.plain: A graph with chromatic number k must have at least one edge between every pair of colour classes, so it has at least C(k,2) = k(k-1)/2 edges - as many as the complete graph K_k.
formulas.whenToUse: To lower-bound the number of edges of a graph given its chromatic number.
formulas.trap: If two colour classes had no edge between them they could be merged into one colour, contradicting chi = k; that is why every one of the C(k,2) class pairs contributes at least one edge.
formulas.reference: Standard argument: a chi=k graph contains K_k as a 'minor of classes', forcing C(k,2) edges.
